1: The chapter opens with wĕʾĕlîšāʿ dibber — ‘Elisha had spoken’ — a pluperfect construction in context (he had previously warned the Shunammite). The reason for urgency: qārāʾ YHWH lārāʿāb — ‘YHWH has called for a famine.’ The Piel of qārāʾ (call, summon) makes YHWH the active agent of the famine — it is a divine summons, not a natural disaster. The seven-year duration (šebaʿ šānîm) echoes Joseph’s seven-year famine (Gen 41:54), framing this as another exodus-type situation requiring prophetic guidance for survival.
2: The woman does exactly as the man of God commanded: wattemah wĕtaʿaś kidĕbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘she arose and did according to the word of the man of God.’ The verb wattēšeb bĕʾereṣ pĕlištîm — ‘she lived in the land of the Philistines.’ The Philistine land as a refuge for an Israelite woman is unexpected; it recalls Ruth’s sojourn in Moab. The narrator notes she was there šebaʿ šānîm — seven years, precisely the famine’s duration.
3: After seven years: wayyāšob hāʾiššāh mēʾereṣ pĕlištîm — ‘the woman returned.’ The verb šûb (return) is theologically loaded in Kings: it is the call to covenant return (tĕšûḇāh), the hope of restoration. The woman goes to the king to appeal for her house and land: wattēṣēʾ liṣʿōq ʾel-hammelek ʾel-bêtāh wĕʾel-śāḏĕhā — ‘she went out to cry out to the king for her house and land.’ The verb ṣāʿaq (cry out) is again the legal appeal to royal justice.
4: The king was talking with Gehazi: wĕhammelek mĕdabbēr ʾel-gēḥăzî naʿar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘the king was talking with Gehazi the servant of the man of God.’ The participial construction (mĕdabbēr) marks the scene as simultaneously occurring with the woman’s arrival. Gehazi’s presence here is unexplained — in ch 5:27 he was struck with leprosy permanently. The narrator does not resolve the tension; some interpreters read this as a flashback (before the leprosy episode) or as a different period. The question remains open in the text.
5: While Gehazi is recounting how Elisha raised the dead child, wĕhinnēh hāʾiššāh ʾăšer-hĕḥěyyāh ʾet-bĕnāh zōʿĕqet ʾel-hammelek — ‘behold, the woman whose son he had raised was crying out to the king.’ The hinnēh (behold) marks the providential coincidence: Gehazi is telling the exact story about the exact woman at the exact moment she arrives. This kind of narrative coincidence (miqqreh) is treated in Kings as YHWH’s invisible orchestration.
6: The king assigns an officer to restore the woman’s property: wĕkol-tĕbûʾōt haśśāḏeh mēʾāz ʿăzōbāh hāʾāreṣ wĕʿad-ʿattāh — ‘all the produce of the field from the time she left the land until now.’ The phrase wĕʿad-ʿattāh (until now) extends the restoration backward in time — the king does not merely return the land but compensates for all income lost during the seven years. This is the OT equivalent of the year of Jubilee’s restoration principle (Lev 25:27-28).
7: Elisha travels to Damascus: wayyābōʾ ʾĕlîšāʿ dammāśeq. The reason for the journey is not given; the man of God moves where YHWH directs. Ben-hadad the king of Syria was ill (ḥōleh). When told ‘the man of God has come here’ (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm bā ʿad-hēnnāh), the king sends Hazael with a gift (minḥāh) — the same word used for tribute and gift-offering in diplomatic and religious contexts.
8: Ben-hadad commands Hazael: qah bĕyāḏĕkā minḥāh wĕlēk liqrāʾt ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm wĕdāraštā ʾet-YHWH mēʾittô — ‘take a gift and go meet the man of God and inquire of YHWH through him.’ The verb dāraš (inquire/seek) is the standard OT term for consulting a prophet: dāraš YHWH (seek YHWH). Ben-hadad, the Aramean king, uses YHWH’s name and seeks his prophet — a recognition that transcends national boundaries, as Naaman’s healing did.
9: Hazael brings a lavish gift: ʿeśrîm gāmāl massāʾ mikkol-ṭûb dammāśeq — ‘forty camel loads of every good thing of Damascus.’ The phrase mikkol-ṭûb (from all the good things) echoes the ‘good of the land’ language in Gen 45:18-20 (Jacob’s arrival in Egypt). Hazael stands before Elisha and calls him binkā ben-hadad, ‘your son Ben-hadad’ — the king addresses the prophet as a superior.
10: Elisha’s double-edged oracle: lēk ʾĕmōr-lô ḥāyōh tiḥyeh — ‘Go tell him, you will certainly recover.’ The infinitive absolute + imperfect construction (ḥāyōh tiḥyeh) expresses certainty: he will definitely recover from the illness. But then: wĕhirʾanî YHWH kî môt yāmût — ‘but YHWH has shown me that he will certainly die.’ The same grammatical certainty (môt yāmût, infinitive absolute + imperfect) applies to his death. The two certainties are not contradictory: Ben-hadad will recover from this illness but will die by another means — murder by Hazael (v15).
11: wayyaʿămēd ʾet-pānāyw wayyāśem ʿad-bōš — ‘he fixed his gaze on him and stared until he was ashamed/embarrassed.’ The verb wayyaʿămēd (made to stand, fixed) with pānāyw (his face) means he held his countenance motionless. The phrase ʿad-bōš (until shame) — Hazael becomes uncomfortable under the prophet’s unseeing gaze. Elisha then wayyēḇk ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — wept. The man of God’s tears are the only explicit prophetic weeping in Kings (cf. Jeremiah’s tears in Lamentations).
12: Hazael asks, maddûaʿ ʾăḏōnî ḇōkeh — ‘why does my lord weep?’ Elisha’s answer catalogues the atrocities: kî yōdaʿtî ʾēt ʾăšer-taʿăśeh liḇnê yiśrāʾēl rāʿāh — ‘because I know the evil you will inflict on Israel.’ The list — burning their fortresses, killing their young men with the sword, dashing their children to pieces (tĕraṭṭēš), ripping open pregnant women — uses verbs that occur in the devastation oracles of Amos (1:13) and Hosea (13:16). The prophet weeps not for Hazael’s future sins but for Israel’s coming suffering.
13: Hazael protests: meh ʿabdĕkā hakkeleb — ‘what is your servant, a mere dog?’ The keleb (dog) self-deprecation formula marks complete lowliness (2 Sam 9:8; 1 Sam 24:14). The rhetorical question kî yaʿăśeh ʿabdĕkā haggedōl hazzeh — ‘that your servant should do this great thing?’ — uses gāḏôl (great) with bitterness: Hazael protests that the deeds are too great (terrible) for a dog like him to commit. Elisha’s answer is blunt: hirʾanî YHWH ʾōtĕkā melek ʿal ʾărām, ‘YHWH has shown me that you will be king over Aram.’
14: Hazael returns to Ben-hadad: wayyāšob ʾel-ʾăḏōnāyw — ‘he returned to his master.’ The king asks, mah ʾāmar lĕkā ʾĕlîšāʿ — ‘What did Elisha say to you?’ Hazael answers partially: ḥāyōh tiḥyeh, ‘you will certainly recover.’ He reports only the first half of Elisha’s oracle and omits the second (môt yāmût, he will certainly die). The selective report is not marked as a lie by the narrator; the reader alone knows what was left unsaid.
15: wayyiqqa miqqreh hammāḥărāt — ‘the next day he took a thick cloth (makbēr) and soaked it in water and spread it over the king’s face, and he died.’ The word makbēr is found only here; likely a thick woven cloth used as a damp smothering agent — not a pillow but a wet cloth blocking respiration. The verb wayyamot (he died) is unadorned: no judgment, no comment, just the bare fact. Hazael’s ascension is narrated in the same verse: wayyimlok ḥăzāʾēl taḥtāyw, ‘and Hazael became king in his place.’
16: The narrative shifts to Judah: ûbišnat ḥāmēš lĕyôrām ben-ʾaḥʾāḇ melek yiśrāʾēl — ‘in the fifth year of Joram son of Ahab king of Israel.’ The synchronistic dating formula (aligning Judahite with Israelite regnal years) is the Kings historian’s standard method of organizing the divided monarchy’s parallel timeline. Malak yĕhôrām ben-yĕhôšāpāṭ melek yĕhûḏāh — Jehoram of Judah begins his reign.
17: ben-šĕlōšîm ûšĕtayim šānāh hāyāh bĕmalĕkô — ‘he was thirty-two years old when he became king.’ The regnal formula follows the standard pattern: age at accession, years of reign, maternal lineage, theological assessment. Jehoram reigned eight years (šmōneh šānîm mālaḵ bîrûšālaim).
18: The theological assessment: wayyēlek bĕderek malkê yiśrāʾēl kaʾăšer ʿāśû bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ — ‘he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as the house of Ahab had done.’ The decisive phrase: kî bat-ʾaḥʾāḇ hāyĕtāh lô lĕʾiššāh, ‘for the daughter of Ahab was his wife.’ The Ahab connection through marriage explains the theological contamination. Hālak bĕderek (walked in the way of) is the Kings historian’s standard negative formula, the inverse of ‘walking in the way of David.’
19: The key theological crux: wĕlōʾ-ʾābāh YHWH lĕhašḥît ʾet-yĕhûḏāh lĕmaʿan dāwîḏ ʿabdô — ‘Yet YHWH was not willing to destroy Judah, for the sake of his servant David.’ The phrase lōʾ-ʾābāh (not willing, refused to consent) is the strongest possible expression of YHWH’s restraint. The ground: lĕmaʿan dāwîḏ ʿabdô, ‘for the sake of his servant David’ — the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16) functions as a limit on divine judgment. The specific promise is quoted: kĕʾăšer ʾāmar lô lātēt lô nîr lĕbānāyw kol-hayyāmîm, ‘as he had promised to give a lamp to him and to his sons for all time.’ The nîr (lamp) is a metaphor for the continuing royal line (1 Kgs 11:36; 15:4).
20: bāyāmîm hāhēm pāšaʿ ʾĕḏôm mittaḥat yad-yĕhûḏāh — ‘In his days Edom revolted from under Judah’s rule.’ The verb pāšaʿ (revolt, rebel, transgress) is used both for political revolt and for covenant transgression. The Edomite revolt fulfills the oracle of Gen 27:40 (Esau will break free from Jacob’s yoke). Edom’s independence is framed as covenant history catching up with itself: the yoke of Jacob/Israel was never permanent.
21: wayyaʿăḇōr yôrām ṣāʿîrāh — ‘Joram marched to Zair.’ He ‘rose up by night and struck the Edomites who surrounded him’ — wayyāqom lāylāh wayyak ʾet-ʾĕḏôm hasson’ēb ʾēlāyw. But the continuation is ambiguous: wayyānos hāʿām lĕʾohŏlāyw, ‘the people fled to their tents.’ It is unclear whether ‘the people’ refers to Joram’s Israelite troops fleeing (military failure) or the Edomites. The ambiguity may be intentional — a botched campaign that neither defeated Edom nor was itself defeated.
22: wayyipšaʿ ʾĕḏôm mittaḥat yad-yĕhûḏāh ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — ‘so Edom has been in revolt against Judah to this day.’ The phrase ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh (to this day) is the Kings narrator’s way of marking a state that persists until the time of writing. Libnah (liḇnāh gam-hîʾ pāšĕʿāh) also revolted ‘at the same time’ (bāʿēt hahîʾ). Two simultaneous revolts under a weak king mark the beginning of Judah’s political contraction.
23: The regnal closing formula: wĕyeter diḇrê yôrām wĕkol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh hălem-hēm kĕtûḇîm ʿal-sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lĕmalkê yĕhûḏāh — ‘the rest of the acts of Joram and everything he did, are they not written in the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Judah?’ This formula, repeated for almost every king, presupposes the reader’s access to other records — the Kings narrative is selective, not comprehensive. The historian has chosen which details serve the theological argument about covenant fidelity and its consequences.
24: wayyiškaḇ yôrām ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw — ‘Joram rested with his ancestors.’ The standard death formula šākab ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw (lay down/slept with his fathers) is used across the Davidic line. The variation wayyiqqāḇēr ʾittām bĕʿîr dāwîḏ — ‘buried with them in the city of David’ — indicates continuation of the royal burial tradition, despite Joram’s apostasy.
25: bišnat šĕtêm ʿeśrēh šānāh lĕyôrām ben-ʾaḥʾāḇ melek yiśrāʾēl — the synchronistic formula anchors Ahaziah of Judah’s accession to year 12 of Israel’s Joram. The two northern and southern reigns are tracked in tandem throughout Kings — the historian’s method of maintaining chronological coherence across the divided monarchy.
26: Ahaziah’s regnal formula: ben-ʿeśrîm ûšĕtayim šānāh — twenty-two years old. Reigned one year (šānāh ʾaḥat mālaḵ bîrûšālaim). His mother: ʿătalyāhû bat-ʿomrî melek yiśrāʾēl, ‘Athaliah the daughter of Omri king of Israel.’ The Omride connection through the maternal line (even more proximate than through his father’s marriage to an Ahab daughter) marks Ahaziah as doubly contaminated by the house of Ahab.
27: wayyēlek bĕderek bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ — ‘he walked in the way of the house of Ahab.’ The phrase is used four times across Jehoram and Ahaziah (vv18, 27): the Ahab contamination of the Davidic line is the historian’s central concern in this section. The theological verdict: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH kaʾăšer bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ — ‘he did evil in the sight of YHWH as the house of Ahab had done.’
28: Political narrative: Ahaziah went with Joram son of Ahab to fight against Hazael king of Syria at Ramoth-gilead. The verb wayyēlek... lammilḥāmāh — ‘went to war.’ The mention of Hazael confirms the fulfillment of Elisha’s oracle (vv13-15): Hazael is now king of Aram and attacking Israel. The narrative loop closes: Elisha prophesied Hazael’s kingship and atrocities; Hazael is now executing them.
29: Joram is wounded: wayyāšob yôrām hammelek lĕhitrappēʾ bĕyizrĕʿēl — ‘King Joram returned to Jezreel to recover from the wounds.’ The verb rāpāʾ (heal, be healed) — Joram retreats to Jezreel, the Omride winter capital and the site of Naboth’s vineyard. The chapter ends with Ahaziah going to Jezreel to visit (lirʾôt, ‘to see’) the wounded Joram — a visit that will lead to his own death in the Jehu narrative (ch 9). The narrative tension is established without resolution.
